questionText: Prove that the product of any four consecutive integers is divisible by $24$.

steps.explanation: Let $P$ be the product of the four consecutive integers starting at $n$. We must show $24 \mid P$.
It suffices to prove divisibility by $8$ and by $3$ separately, since $8$ and $3$ share no common factor.

steps.description: Divisibility by 3 — locate a multiple of 3.
steps.explanation: Any three consecutive integers contain a multiple of $3$, and $n,n+1,n+2$ are among our four factors.
Checking each remainder confirms one of the first three factors is always divisible by $3$, so $3 \mid P$.
In every case a factor is a multiple of $3$, so the whole product is.

steps.description: Divisibility by 8 — count the even factors.
steps.workingLatex: \text{among 4 consecutive integers, exactly two are even}
steps.explanation: Consecutive integers alternate parity, so among four in a row precisely two are even.
The two even factors differ by $2$, so they are consecutive even numbers, writable as $2m$ and $2m+2$.
Each even factor contributes a $2$, giving an explicit factor of $4$ times $m(m+1)$.

steps.description: Use consecutiveness of m and m+1.
steps.explanation: Among two consecutive integers one is even, so $m(m+1)$ is even, say $2t$.

steps.description: Conclude the even part is divisible by 8.
steps.explanation: Substituting $m(m+1)=2t$ shows the product of the two even factors is divisible by $8$.
Since these two even numbers are factors of $P$, and their product is divisible by $8$, so is $P$.
We have now established divisibility by both $8$ and $3$.
Because $8$ and $3$ are coprime, divisibility by each separately forces divisibility by their product.
Combining the coprime factors gives $8 \times 3 = 24$ dividing $P$.
Hence the product of any four consecutive integers is divisible by $24$.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: Among four consecutive integers one of $n,n+1,n+2$ is a multiple of $3$, and the two even factors are consecutive evens whose product is $8t$. So $3\mid P$ and $8\mid P$; as $\gcd(8,3)=1$, $24\mid P$. $\blacksquare$
